Fitting occupancy models

Analysing Ecological Data with Detection Error

Author

Péter Sólymos

Published

April 21, 2026

Preamble

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
    library(unmarked)
    library(detect)
    library(dplyr)
    library(ggplot2)
    theme_set(theme_light())
})
set.seed(1234)

Simulating occupancy data

Let’s see how we can use the Bernoulli distribution and the rbinom function to simulate occupancy data.

Let us sample n=50 locations and observe the occupancy status of Ovenbirds at each of the locations:

  • The true occupancy status at the ith location is W_{i}.
  • The probability of occupancy is P(W_{i}=1)=\phi.

The true occupancy probability \phi is denoted with phi.true:

n <- 50
phi.true <- 0.4

W <- rbinom(n = n, size = 1, prob = phi.true)

table(W = W)
W
 0  1 
31 19

There are many reasons why we might not observe the true occupancy status W. When the data that we collect (Y) is not the true occupancy status W, we say that there is detection error. It can manifest the following way:

We can capture detection error also as a probability p which is the probability of detecting the species when present (W_i=1).

  • If the species is absent (W_i=0), we will always observe 0, thus Y_i=0: P(Y_i=0|W_{i}=0)=1.
  • If W_i=1, we might observe 0 (missed all the birds) or 1:
    • species detected when present, P(Y_i=1|W_{i}=1)=p
    • species not detected when present, P(Y_i=0|W_{i}=1)=1-p

Let’s simulate what we observe, the Y vector. We use the rbinom() function with n = n as before, but the size argument is not a fixed 1, but the true status W. When W is 0, the function will always return 0, when W is 1, the function will reyrun 1 or 0 according the the detection probability p, denoted as p.true:

p.true <- 0.6
Y <- rbinom(n = n, size = W, prob = p.true)

table(W = W, Y = Y)
   Y
W    0  1
  0 31  0
  1  5 14

The cross-tabulation show how many times we observed 1’s vs 0’s when the true status was 1.

Using the observations as if those would represent the true status is called the naive approach. The naive approach assume that there is no observation error:

mean(Y)
[1] 0.28

But we see that the mean of Y is quite far from phi.true.

Fitting a logistic regression (aka Binomial GLM) to the data yields the same result:

m1 <- glm(Y ~ 1, family = binomial)
coef(m1)
(Intercept) 
 -0.9444616 
plogis(coef(m1))
(Intercept) 
       0.28

The coefficient for the intercept is the maximum likelihood estimate on the logit scale. To transform that to the 0-1 probability scale, we can use the plogis() function that implements the inverse logistic transformation.

The unobserved W variable is often called a latent variable. It is latent in the sense that it cannot be directly observed. We need to find ways to:

  • use survey design to minimize the detection error, or
  • use statistical models to correct for detection error, or
  • the combination of the 2.

Simulating multiple visits

A feature of multiple-visit methods is that we visit the same site T times. Each visit (t=1,\ldots,T) gives us a different observation, Y_{it}.

Key assumptions are the following:

  • the visits are independent of each other
  • the true status W stays the same over the visits (W_i=W_{it})

We can generalize the simulation code to any number of T visits as:

T <- 5
Y <- matrix(NA, n, T)
for (t in 1:T) {
    Y[, t] <- rbinom(n = n, size = W, prob = p.true)
}

A more concise way of writing the above code without a loop is:

Y <- replicate(T, rbinom(n = n, size = W, prob = p.true))

If we inspect any row (site) from the Y matrix, we see a series of 1’s and 0’s for sites where the species is present. This is called the detection history.

data.frame(
    W = head(W[W > 0]),
    Visit = head(Y[W > 0, ])
)
  W Visit.1 Visit.2 Visit.3 Visit.4 Visit.5
1 1       1       1       1       1       0
2 1       1       0       1       1       0
3 1       0       1       1       1       1
4 1       1       0       0       1       0
5 1       0       1       1       0       1
6 1       1       1       0       0       1

The detection history is all 0’s for sites where the species is absent:

data.frame(
    W = head(W[W == 0]),
    Visit = head(Y[W == 0, ])
)
  W Visit.1 Visit.2 Visit.3 Visit.4 Visit.5
1 0       0       0       0       0       0
2 0       0       0       0       0       0
3 0       0       0       0       0       0
4 0       0       0       0       0       0
5 0       0       0       0       0       0
6 0       0       0       0       0       0

We can compare the maximum of the observed values over the visits at each site:

Y_max <- apply(Y, 1, max)
table(W = W, Y_max = Y_max)
   Y_max
W    0  1
  0 31  0
  1  0 19

Fit the logistic regression model to Y_max:

m2 <- glm(Y_max ~ 1, family = binomial)
coef(m2)
(Intercept) 
 -0.4895482 
plogis(coef(m2))
(Intercept) 
       0.38
TipWhat happens when we increase the number of visits?

Change the value of T and compare the Y_max to W. What happens to our naive estimator of using Y_max?

The maximum over a large number of visits will approach the latent variable W:

n2 <- 500
W2 <- rbinom(n = n2, size = 1, prob = phi.true)
Tvals <- 1:10

data.frame(
    T = Tvals,
    Y_max = sapply(Tvals, \(T) {
        mean(apply(
            replicate(T, rbinom(n = n2, size = W, prob = p.true)),
            1, max
        ))
    })
) |> ggplot(aes(x = T, y = Y_max)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = phi.true, lty = 2, col = "red") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = mean(W), lty = 2, col = "blue") +
    geom_line() +
    ylim(0, 1) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = Tvals) +
    theme_light()

Fitting occupancy models

The unmarked package implements the multiple-visit occupancy model.

CautionReferences

MacKenzie et al., 2002. Estimating site occupancy rates when detection probabilities are less than one. Ecology, 83:2248–2255. Fulltext, DOI 10.1890/0012-9658(2002)083[2248:ESORWD]2.0.CO;2

  • Organize the data and an unmarked occupancy data frame
  • Fit the model with the occu
  • the ~1 ~1 formula says that we do not have covariates, just the intercepts
umf <- unmarked::unmarkedFrameOccu(y = Y)
summary(umf)
unmarkedFrame Object

50 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 5 
Mean number of observations per site: 5 
Sites with at least one detection: 19 

Tabulation of y observations:
  0   1 
186  64 
m3 <- unmarked::occu(~1 ~ 1, umf)
m3

Call:
unmarked::occu(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf)

Occupancy (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE     z P(>|z|)
   -0.483 0.292 -1.65   0.098

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE    z P(>|z|)
    0.713 0.222 3.21 0.00134

AIC: 190.2551 
Number of sites: 50

We use the plogis() function again to transform the estimates to the probability scale and compare with our phi.true and p.true values:

plogis(coef(m3, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.6710909 
plogis(coef(m3, type = "state"))
 psi(Int) 
0.3814687

Maximum likelihood estimator

The glm() and occu() functions use maximum likelihood to find the parameter estimates for a given data set. In other words, we coefficients (maximum likelihood estimate, or MLE) maximize the likelihood function for the data set in question. The likelihood function can be relatively simple and easy to calculate, or it can be computationally challenging to compute (e.g. for hierarchical or mixed models).

The likelihood function L for the simple occupancy model with parameters p and \phi for multiple visits data can be written as:

L(p, \phi; y_{1,1}, \ldots, y_{n,T}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left[ \phi \left( \binom{T}{y_{i \cdot}} p^{y_{i \cdot}} (1 - p)^{T - y_{i \cdot}} \right) + (1 - \phi) I(y_{i \cdot} = 0)\right]

where y_{i \cdot} = \sum^{t=1}_{T} y_{i,t} and I( y_{i \cdot} = 0 ) is an indicator function that is equal to 1 if y_{i \cdot} = 0.

Here is the R code to calculate the log likelihood for the occupancy model:

L_fun_occu <- function(Y, p, phi) {
    ydot <- rowSums(Y)
    T <- ncol(Y)
    L <- prod(
        phi *
            (choose(T, ydot) * p^ydot * (1 - p)^(T - ydot)) +
            (1 - phi) * (ydot == 0)
    )
    L
}

Next, we evaluate the likelihood function at different values of p and phi while keeping the data Y constant. We set up the grid for this using expand.grid():

g <- 100
grid <- expand.grid(
    p = seq(0, 1, length.out = g),
    phi = seq(0, 1, length.out = g),
    L = NA
)

for (i in 1:nrow(grid)) {
    grid$L[i] <- L_fun_occu(
        Y = Y,
        p = grid$p[i],
        phi = grid$phi[i]
    )
}

When we plot the likelihood surface, we see the maximum, the lines indicate the true probability values:

image(
    list(
        x = unique(grid$p),
        y = unique(grid$phi),
        z = matrix(grid$L, g, g)
    ),
    xlab = "p",
    ylab = expression(varphi)
)
abline(h = phi.true, v = p.true, col = 1, lwd = 1)
grid |> ggplot(aes(x = p, y = phi, z = L)) +
    geom_contour_filled(show.legend = FALSE) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = phi.true) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = p.true) +
    xlab("p") +
    ylab(expression(phi)) +
    theme_light()
grid[which.max(grid$L), ]
             p       phi            L
3867 0.6666667 0.3838384 2.234106e-27

If the rgl package is installed, we can view the surface in 3D:

if (interactive()) {
    library(rgl)

    L_mat <- matrix(grid$L, g, g)
    dcpal_grbu <- colorRampPalette(c("#18bc9c", "#3498db"))
    Col <- rev(dcpal_grbu(12))[cut(L_mat, breaks = 12)]

    open3d()
    persp3d(
        x = unique(grid$p),
        y = unique(grid$phi),
        z = L_mat / max(L_mat),
        col = Col,
        theta = 50, phi = 25, expand = 0.75, ticktype = "detailed",
        xlab = "p", ylab = expression(phi), zlab = "L"
    )
    quads3d(
        x = rep(p.true, 4),
        y = c(0, 0, 1, 1),
        z = c(0, 1, 1, 0),
        alpha = 0.5, col = 2
    )
    quads3d(
        x = c(0, 0, 1, 1),
        y = rep(phi.true, 4),
        z = c(0, 1, 1, 0),
        alpha = 0.5, col = 4
    )
}
Note

We will circle back to these plots later, feel free to explore it with different settings of n, T, p, and \phi.

Field data analysis

We will use the ABMI data, which has 3 equal (3.3 min) time intervals for each point count visit. We use the point level data, but only for the center point, so we can keep sample size small and run the code quickly. When analyzing such data sets for real, we would use all the data and not subset it like this.

if (!interactive()) {
    d <- readRDS("../data/ab_birds_site_year_pt.rds")
} else {
    d <- readRDS("data/ab_birds_site_year_pt.rds")
}

spp <- "yellowrumped_warbler"

x <- data.frame(
    y1 = ifelse(d$visits$visit1[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    y2 = ifelse(d$visits$visit2[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    y3 = ifelse(d$visits$visit3[, spp] > 0, 1, 0),
    n1 = d$visits$visit1[, spp],
    n2 = d$visits$visit2[, spp],
    n3 = d$visits$visit3[, spp],
    d$vars
)
x$yday <- as.POSIXlt(x$fielddate)$yday / 365
x$hr <- x$fieldtime / 24
x$y_max <- pmax(x$y1, x$y2, x$y3)
x$n_max <- pmax(x$n1, x$n2, x$n3)
x <- x |>
    filter(
        hr > 0.1,
        hr < 0.6,
        yday > 0.37,
        yday < 0.5
    )
x0 <- x[x$point == 1, ]

Naive occupancy estimates for the 3 visits:

m_v1 <- glm(y1 ~ 1, data = x0, family = binomial)
coef(m_v1)
(Intercept) 
  -1.076354 
plogis(coef(m_v1))
(Intercept) 
  0.2541966 
mean(x0$y1)
[1] 0.2541966
m_v2 <- glm(y2 ~ 1, data = x0, family = binomial)
coef(m_v2)
(Intercept) 
  -1.121121 
plogis(coef(m_v2))
(Intercept) 
  0.2458034 
mean(x0$y2)
[1] 0.2458034
m_v3 <- glm(y3 ~ 1, data = x0, family = binomial)
coef(m_v3)
(Intercept) 
  -1.147166 
plogis(coef(m_v3))
(Intercept) 
  0.2410072 
mean(x0$y3)
[1] 0.2410072

Given that the 3 subsequent ‘visits’ are actually time intervals within the same point count, we can safely assume that the true occupancy status does not change between the visits (closed population assumption). Therefore, we can use the y_max variable to fit a naive occupancy model to approximate the true occupancy status:

m_max <- glm(y_max ~ 1, data = x0, family = binomial)
coef(m_max)
(Intercept) 
 -0.7696873 
plogis(coef(m_max))
(Intercept) 
  0.3165468 
mean(x0$y_max)
[1] 0.3165468

Question: What do you think about the closed population assumption? What if visits are days, weeks, months, or years apart?

Let’s fit the occupancy model to the data using all 3 visits:

umf <- unmarked::unmarkedFrameOccu(y = x0[, c("y1", "y2", "y3")])
summary(umf)
unmarkedFrame Object

834 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 3 
Mean number of observations per site: 3 
Sites with at least one detection: 264 

Tabulation of y observations:
   0    1 
1884  618 
m_mv <- unmarked::occu(~1 ~ 1, umf)
m_mv

Call:
unmarked::occu(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf)

Occupancy (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE   z  P(>|z|)
   -0.752 0.075 -10 1.11e-23

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate     SE    z  P(>|z|)
     1.21 0.0897 13.5 1.14e-41

AIC: 1873.258 
Number of sites: 834
plogis(coef(m_mv, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.7709228 
plogis(coef(m_mv, type = "state"))
 psi(Int) 
0.3203975

Use covariates: for status and detection

umf2 <- unmarked::unmarkedFrameOccu(
    y = x0[, c("y1", "y2", "y3")],
    siteCovs = x0[, c("pconif", "thf")],
    obsCovs = list(
        hr = x0[, c("hr", "hr", "hr")],
        yday = x0[, c("yday", "yday", "yday")]
    )
)
summary(umf2)
unmarkedFrame Object

834 sites
Maximum number of observations per site: 3 
Mean number of observations per site: 3 
Sites with at least one detection: 264 

Tabulation of y observations:
   0    1 
1884  618 

Site-level covariates:
     pconif            thf         
 Min.   :0.0000   Min.   :0.00000  
 1st Qu.:0.0000   1st Qu.:0.00000  
 Median :0.0000   Median :0.06333  
 Mean   :0.2740   Mean   :0.34896  
 3rd Qu.:0.6036   3rd Qu.:0.86650  
 Max.   :1.0000   Max.   :1.00000  

Observation-level covariates:
       hr              yday       
 Min.   :0.1493   Min.   :0.3890  
 1st Qu.:0.2111   1st Qu.:0.4082  
 Median :0.2257   Median :0.4192  
 Mean   :0.2516   Mean   :0.4273  
 3rd Qu.:0.2562   3rd Qu.:0.4493  
 Max.   :0.5271   Max.   :0.4822  
m_mv2 <- unmarked::occu(
    ~ hr + I(hr^2) + yday ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + thf,
    umf2
)
m_mv2

Call:
unmarked::occu(formula = ~hr + I(hr^2) + yday ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + 
    thf, data = umf2)

Occupancy (logit-scale):
            Estimate    SE     z  P(>|z|)
(Intercept)    -1.05 0.154 -6.81 1.01e-11
pconif          5.67 0.988  5.74 9.28e-09
I(pconif^2)    -3.97 1.010 -3.93 8.43e-05
thf            -2.18 0.324 -6.74 1.63e-11

Detection (logit-scale):
            Estimate    SE      z P(>|z|)
(Intercept)     5.09  2.25  2.260  0.0239
hr             -5.46  9.85 -0.554  0.5795
I(hr^2)         9.74 15.55  0.626  0.5312
yday           -7.45  4.18 -1.781  0.0749

AIC: 1618.481 
Number of sites: 834
Warning: Large or missing SE values. Be very cautious using these results.
m_mv@AIC
[1] 1873.258
m_mv2@AIC
[1] 1618.481

Fitted values reflect expected values of the marginal distribution, i.e. combining the state and detection processes:

summary(fitted(m_mv2))
       V1                V2                V3         
 Min.   :0.02592   Min.   :0.02592   Min.   :0.02592  
 1st Qu.:0.04205   1st Qu.:0.04205   1st Qu.:0.04205  
 Median :0.19161   Median :0.19161   Median :0.19161  
 Mean   :0.24616   Mean   :0.24616   Mean   :0.24616  
 3rd Qu.:0.47832   3rd Qu.:0.47832   3rd Qu.:0.47832  
 Max.   :0.59388   Max.   :0.59388   Max.   :0.59388  
colMeans(umf2@y)
       y1        y2        y3 
0.2541966 0.2458034 0.2410072

We can calculate the expected occupancy probability for each site by using the coefficients of the model matrix:

f <- m_mv2@formula[-2]
f
~pconif + I(pconif^2) + thf
X <- model.matrix(f, x0)
head(X)
            (Intercept)    pconif I(pconif^2)        thf
1_2009_1              1 0.6440748   0.4148324 0.00000000
1001_2011_1           1 1.0000000   1.0000000 0.74507161
1002_2011_1           1 0.8230018   0.6773319 0.12879228
1003_2011_1           1 0.9614613   0.9244079 0.03853867
1007_2008_1           1 0.9644391   0.9301428 0.03556088
1008_2008_1           1 0.6248039   0.3903800 0.17998054
coef(m_mv2, type = "state")
        psi(Int)      psi(pconif) psi(I(pconif^2))         psi(thf) 
       -1.048719         5.674886        -3.972183        -2.184805 
X[1, ] * coef(m_mv2, type = "state")
(Intercept)      pconif I(pconif^2)         thf 
  -1.048719    3.655051   -1.647790    0.000000 
sum(X[1, ] * coef(m_mv2, type = "state"))
[1] 0.9585418
head(X %*% coef(m_mv2, type = "state"), 1)
              [,1]
1_2009_1 0.9585418
fit_mv2 <- plogis(X %*% coef(m_mv2, type = "state"))
summary(fit_mv2)
       V1         
 Min.   :0.03792  
 1st Qu.:0.05665  
 Median :0.25650  
 Mean   :0.32101  
 3rd Qu.:0.64404  
 Max.   :0.72674  
hist(fit_mv2)

Visualize the fitted occupancy probabilities with respect to the covariates:

data.frame(x0, p_occ = fit_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = pconif, y = p_occ, col = thf)) +
    geom_point() +
    ylim(0, 1)
data.frame(x0, p_occ = fit_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = as.factor(y_max), y = p_occ)) +
    geom_boxplot() +
    ylim(0, 1)
m_max2 <- glm(y_max ~ pconif + I(pconif^2) + thf, data = x0, family = binomial)

data.frame(p_occ = fit_mv2, p_max = fitted(m_max2)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = p_max, y = p_occ)) +
    geom_point() +
    geom_abline(lty = 2) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
mean(fit_mv2 / fitted(m_max2))
[1] 1.012492

Predict detection probability given detection covariates:

Z <- model.matrix(m_mv2@formula[-3], x0)
head(Z)
            (Intercept)        hr    I(hr^2)      yday
1_2009_1              1 0.2354167 0.05542101 0.4410959
1001_2011_1           1 0.2437500 0.05941406 0.4465753
1002_2011_1           1 0.2465278 0.06077595 0.4356164
1003_2011_1           1 0.3888889 0.15123457 0.4383562
1007_2008_1           1 0.2666667 0.07111111 0.4054795
1008_2008_1           1 0.2020833 0.04083767 0.4164384
det_mv2 <- plogis(Z %*% coef(m_mv2, type = "det"))
summary(det_mv2)
       V1        
 Min.   :0.6802  
 1st Qu.:0.7368  
 Median :0.7761  
 Mean   :0.7661  
 3rd Qu.:0.7923  
 Max.   :0.8572  
hist(det_mv2)
data.frame(x0, p_det = det_mv2) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = yday, y = p_det, col = hr)) +
    geom_point() +
    ylim(0, 1)

Question: How would we assess model fit? Can we use AUC/ROC?

What do we get when only using 2 visits?

umf_v2 <- unmarked::unmarkedFrameOccu(y = x0[, c("y1", "y2")])
m_mv_v2 <- unmarked::occu(~1 ~ 1, umf_v2)
m_mv_v2

Call:
unmarked::occu(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf_v2)

Occupancy (logit-scale):
 Estimate     SE     z  P(>|z|)
   -0.849 0.0777 -10.9 8.01e-28

Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate    SE    z  P(>|z|)
     1.62 0.142 11.4 5.66e-30

AIC: 1396.047 
Number of sites: 834
plogis(coef(m_mv_v2, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.8345324 
plogis(coef(m_mv_v2, type = "state"))
psi(Int) 
0.299569

How about only using 1 visit?

umf_v1 <- unmarked::unmarkedFrameOccu(y = x0[, c("y1"), drop = FALSE])
m_mv_v1 <- unmarked::occu(~1 ~ 1, umf_v1)
m_mv_v1

Call:
unmarked::occu(formula = ~1 ~ 1, data = umf_v1)
Warning in sqrt(diag(vcov(obj, fixedOnly = fixedOnly))): NaNs produced
Occupancy (logit-scale):
 Estimate  SE   z P(>|z|)
   0.0167 NaN NaN     NaN
Warning in sqrt(diag(vcov(obj, fixedOnly = fixedOnly))): NaNs produced
Detection (logit-scale):
 Estimate  SE   z P(>|z|)
   0.0167 NaN NaN     NaN

AIC: 949.5873 
Number of sites: 834
Warning in sqrt(diag(v)): NaNs produced
Warning: Large or missing SE values. Be very cautious using these results.
plogis(coef(m_mv_v1, type = "det"))
   p(Int) 
0.5041792 
plogis(coef(m_mv_v1, type = "state"))
 psi(Int) 
0.5041792

Something is wrong here, right? Let put this aside for now, we will come back to this later.
